blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلشَّمْسِ وَضُحَىٰهَا (۱)
By the sun and her forenoon splendour, (1)
وَٱلْقَمَرِ إِذَا تَلَىٰهَا (۲)
by the moon when he follows her, (2)
وَٱلنَّهَارِ إِذَا جَلَّىٰهَا (۳)
by the day when it reveals her, (3)
وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذَا يَغْشَىٰهَا (٤)
by the night when it covers her, (4)
وَٱلسَّمَآءِ وَمَا بَنَىٰهَا (٥)
by the sky and Him who built it, (5)
وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا طَحَىٰهَا (٦)
by the earth and Him who spread it, (6)
وَنَفْسٍ وَمَا سَوَّىٰهَا (۷)
by the soul and Him who fashioned it, (7)
فَأَلْهَمَهَا فُجُورَهَا وَتَقْوَىٰهَا (۸)
and inspired it with [discernment between] its virtues and vices: (8)

Key Points

Perhaps the secret of swearing by the sun, the moon, the night, the day, and so on, is that the subject for which the oath is taken (purification of the soul) is as valuable as the greatness of the sun and the moon.

The value of the sun, the moon, the night, and the day is hidden from no one. The life of creatures, the formation of clouds, the falling of rain, the growth of plants and trees, and the nourishment of animals and humans are all indebted to the shining of the sun.

In the Quran, sometimes an oath is sworn by the great sun, and sometimes by the small fig and olive. This may be because, for the power of Allah, there is no difference between the sun and the fig. Just as seeing a straw and a mountain is the same for the human eye, and seeing a mountain does not require extra effort beyond seeing a straw.

Of course, the highest oaths are those sworn by the Essence of Allah, especially concerning the submission of people to heavenly leadership. «فلا و ربّك لا یؤمنون حتّى یحكموك» (66)

The Sūrah with the most oaths is this very Sūrah Ash-Shams, which naturally must concern the most important issues, namely the purification of the soul from all kinds of filth and contamination.

Perhaps all these oaths, intended to state that salvation lies in refinement (of the soul), are meant for Allah to make man understand: "I have prepared all the prerequisites for you. With the light of the sun and the moon, I illuminated the stage of life, and with the rotation of night and day, I provided the opportunity for your effort and rest. I spread the earth for you and raised the sky, and I inspired the understanding of good and evil into your soul so that you may, by your own choice, tread the path of self-purification."

The cloud, the wind, the mist, the sun, and the heavens are all at work, So that you may obtain a loaf of bread and not eat it heedlessly. All are bewildered and obedient for your sake; It would not be fair if you were not obedient.

Fujūruhā (فجورها) comes from fajr, meaning to split or cleave.

The raising of the heavens and the spreading of the earth occur either through direct Divine Will or through natural forces and factors. Therefore, the Quran says: «والسماء و ما بناها» (By the heaven and what built it) and did not say man banāhā (who built it), because the word mā (ما) also includes things other than Allah. That is, what built the heaven consists of the natural forces, attractions, and repulsions that Allah has governed. And if the intent of the word mā in «و ما بناها» is Allah, it is because the word mā contains a kind of ambiguity arising from inherent greatness, and for this reason, the word mā is used instead of the word man (who). (And Allah knows best.)

The universe seems to have initially been a single compacted mass. Then, a part was raised and became the sky, and a part was spread out and became the earth. Banāhā (بناها) - Tahāhā (طحاها). Supporting this statement is the verse which says: The heavens and the earth were joined together (ratqan), and We clove them asunder (fataqnāhumā), meaning they were closed and We opened them. «رتقاً ففتقناهما» (67)

Some have considered the word Tahāhā (طحاها) to refer to the movement of the earth, since one of the meanings of tahw is to drive or move.

Islam has moved the materialistic human mind away from the belly, lust, sword, and wine, toward contemplation on the depths of the heavens and the earth, the rotation of the spheres, and the creation of the days.

Regarding the difference between waḥy (revelation) and ilhām (inspiration), it has been said that inspiration is a perception whose source man does not know, whereas the recipient of revelation knows the source of the revelation. (68)

66) Sūrah An-Nisā, 65. 67) Sūrah Al-Anbiyā, 30. 68) Tafsir Namūneh.

Messages

  1. Many existing creatures are wondrous, illuminating, sacred, and valuable phenomena, and oaths may be sworn by them. «والشمس - والقمر - والنهار»
  2. The moon follows the sun and is subordinate to it. «اذا تلاها»
  3. Celestial bodies are interconnected. «و الشمس - والقمر - و السماء - و الارض»
  4. Do not consider the sun, moon, and stars to be God; they are all signs of the power and planning of another Being. «تلاها - بناها - طحاها»
  5. Human desires, inclinations, and instincts have been wisely balanced. «و نفس و ما سوّاها»
  6. Sin is a kind of cleaving (splitting) the veil of chastity and piety. «فجورها»
  7. The understanding and perception of good and evil have been placed inherently (by nature) deep within the human soul. «فالهمها فجورها وتقواها»
  8. First, we must recognize impurities, and then the way to escape and avoid them. (The word fujūr (فجور) precedes taqwā (تقوا).) «فجورها وتقواها»

Explanations

Portrait of Sūrah Ash-Shams

Sūrah Ash-Shams has fifteen verses and was revealed in Mecca. Its name is derived from the first verse, which begins with an oath by the sun.

The greatest number of consecutive oaths in the Quran appear in this Sūrah, emphasizing the value and importance of self-purification through eleven successive oaths by the sun and the moon, the day and the night, and the heaven and the earth.

The theme of this Sūrah is that man, through Divine inspiration, distinguishes good deeds from evil deeds, and if he wishes to attain salvation, he must purify his inner self and nurture it by performing good deeds; otherwise, he will be deprived of felicity. (65)

Then, as evidence, it mentions the story of the people of Thamūd, who were afflicted by a severe punishment because they denied their Prophet, Ḥaḍrat Ṣāliḥ, and killed the she-camel which was a Divine miracle.

65) Tafsir Al-Mīzān.

Ayahs

قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَن زَكَّىٰهَا (۹)
one who purifies it is felicitous, (9)
وَقَدْ خَابَ مَن دَسَّىٰهَا (۱٠)
and one who betrays it fails. (10)
كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ بِطَغْوَىٰهَآ (۱۱)
The [people of] Thamud denied [Allah’s signs] out of their rebellion, (11)
إِذِ ٱنۢبَعَثَ أَشْقَىٰهَا (۱۲)
when the most wretched of them rose up. (12)
فَقَالَ لَهُمْ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ نَاقَةَ ٱللَّهِ وَسُقْيَـٰهَا (۱۳)
The apostle of Allah had told them, ‘This is Allah’s she-camel, let her drink!’ (13)
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَعَقَرُوهَا فَدَمْدَمَ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّهُم بِذَنۢبِهِمْ فَسَوَّىٰهَا (۱٤)
But they impugned him and hamstrung her. So their Lord took them unawares by night because of their sin, and levelled it. (14)
وَلَا يَخَافُ عُقْبَـٰهَا (۱٥)
And He does not fear its outcome. (15)

Key Points

Imam Baqir and Imam Sadiq علیهما السلام, in interpreting the Ayah, "قد افلح من زكّاها" (He has succeeded who purifies it), said: "قد افلح من اطاعَ" (He has succeeded who obeys) (69). Meaning, the one who obeyed the Prophet and the infallible Imams achieved success. In the Divine view, the pure ones are successful. "قد افلح من زكّاها". But in the view of the Pharaohs, the powerful ones who dominate and are outwardly victorious are successful. "قد افلح الیوم من استعلى" (He has succeeded today who gains the upper hand). In performing good deeds, there is hope for success. "و افعلوا الخیر لعلّكم تفلحون" (And do good that you may succeed) (70). Whereas in the purification of the soul (tazkiyah al-nafs) and self-building, success is certain. "قد افلح من زكّاها". The purification of the soul includes distancing oneself from undesirable beliefs, morals, and behaviors, and acquiring praiseworthy beliefs, morals, and deeds. In Surah Ash-Shu'ara, Ayah 155, "لها شرب و لكم شرب یوم معلوم" (She shall have her share of the water, and you shall have your share of the water on a known day), and Surah Al-Qamar, Ayah 28, "نبّئهم انّ الماء قسمة بینهم كلّ شرب محتضر" (And inform them that the water is to be shared between them, each drink being attended in turn), we read: The water of the village is the share of the people one day, and the share of the she-camel (Naqah) the next day, and each shall take their portion on their designated day. No one should interfere with the she-camel, lest they be afflicted by a great punishment. The root word falah (success/salvation) is used forty times in the Quran. By examining these instances, it becomes clear which groups achieve success and which groups are deprived of reaching it, such as the criminals, about whom He says: "لا یفلح المجرمون" (The criminals will not succeed) (71). In the Quran, some matters are introduced with a single oath. "والعصر انّ الانسان لفى خسر" (By the time, indeed, mankind is in loss). Sometimes two oaths follow one another. "والضحى والّیل اذا سَجى" (By the morning brightness, and [by] the night when it covers). Sometimes three oaths follow in succession: "والعادیات ضَبحا، فالموریات قدحا، فالمغیرات صُبحا" (By the snorting chargers, striking sparks of fire, and scouring to the raid at dawn). Sometimes four oaths: "والتین و الزیتون و طور سینین و هذا البلد الامین" (By the fig and the olive, and [by] Mount Sinai, and [by] this secure city). Sometimes five oaths: "والفجر، ولیال عشر، والشفع والوتر، والّیل اذا یسر" (By the dawn, and [by] ten nights, and [by] the even and the odd, and [by] the night when it passes). But in this Surah, Allah first takes eleven oaths and then points to the importance of the purification of the soul. Khaba (خاب) is derived from the root Khaybah (خیبة), meaning failing to reach the goal. Dass (دسّ) means to conceal or hide. The Quranic expression regarding the burying alive of daughters is: "یدسّه فى التراب" (He hides her in the dust). Indeed, the wrongdoer, due to shame, has no face to show in society and tries to conceal his faults and wrongdoings. The word dasisah (دسیسه) is also used for an evil deed that is concealed. Damdama (دَمدم) means a crushing and comprehensive punishment that shatters and scatters the punished. The intent of "سوّاها" (leveled it) is either the destruction of the buildings, which were leveled to the ground, or the people themselves, who were leveled to the ground. A purified individual can be the source of transformation in society, granting the community growth, courage, character, knowledge, and unity, just as an unpurified person, in pursuit of his desires, drags nations toward corruption, destruction, and downfall. In the Quran, two factors are presented for human success: one is faith (Iman) and the other is purification (Tazkiyah). "قد افلح من زكاها" (He has succeeded who purifies it), "قد افلح المؤمنون" (Certainly will the believers have succeeded). Therefore, the reality of faith and purification is one, and whoever wishes to build himself must be a true believer. Indeed, a true believer is one who purifies himself, and a truly purified person is one who has faith and meticulously performs the religious rulings and duties, as stated at the beginning of Surah Al-Mu'minun. We read in Tafsir Atayab al-Bayan that there were many criminals and murderers throughout history, but they usually committed crimes to maintain power, seize power, eliminate a rival, or out of greed for some gain. However, the killer of Salih's she-camel (Naqah) and the killer of Hadrat Ali عليه السلام committed immense crimes when none of the aforementioned motives or factors were involved; it was solely and purely wretchedness (Shaqawah). Hadrat Ali عليه السلام said on the pulpit in Kufa: One person hamstrung Salih's camel, but Allah punished everyone because they were all pleased with the act, and then He referred to this Ayah (72). In numerous narrations from both Shia and Sunni sources, including Ahmad Hanbal, Tabari, and Qurtubi, it is stated that the "most wretched of the former people" (اشقى الاولین) was the hamstrigner of the she-camel, and the "most wretched of the latter people" (اشقى الاخرین) was the killer of Ali ibn Abi Talib (73).

69) Tafsir Noor Ath-Thaqalayn. 70) Al-Hajj, 77. 71) Yunus, 17. 72) Nahj al-Balaghah, Sermon 20. 73) Tafsir Namuneh.

Messages

1- Swearing by the sun, the moon, the night, the day, the earth, the sky, and the human soul indicates that human happiness depends on spiritual growth, not material life. "والشمس... قد افلح من زكّاها".

2- If we abandon the soul (nafs), it leads man to downfall. Therefore, the soul must be controlled. "زكّاها".

3- Purification (Tazkiyah) and self-building is not deprivation; it is growth. "زكّاها".

4- Whoever conceals his soul amidst habits, customs, and delusions, and heedlessly pursues the fulfillment of carnal desires, will suffer great deprivation. "قد خاب من دساها".

5- Transgression (Tughyan) and sin are the prelude to disbelief (Kufr) and denial. "كذّبت ثمود بطغواها" (Thamud denied [the truth] by reason of their transgression). (Taqwa means purity, and Tughwa means transgression in sin).

6- In the method of propagation, after stating general principles, cite examples. "قد خاب... كذّبت ثمود".

7- Violating sacred things is a sign of wretchedness (Shaqawah), and the greater the sanctity, the more cruelty is required to violate it. "اشقى" (the most wretched).

8- If a person does not focus on purification, he initially follows his soul secretly "دسّاها" (concealed it), and then openly. "اذ انبعث اشقاها" (When the most wretched of them was incited).

9- In committing evil deeds, the one who is more wretched is quicker to be incited. "انبعث اشقاها" (74).

10- Whatever is attributed to Allah is sacred and must be respected, and insulting it leads to Divine punishment. "ناقة اللّه" (the she-camel of Allah), "فعقروها" (they hamstrung her), "فدمدم" (He destroyed them).

11- Removing weeds is not enough; fertilization (nurturing) is also necessary. (Zakkaha means growth/nurturing.)

12- Do not be deceived by the fleeting successes of the soul, for their consequence is deprivation. "و قد خاب" (And he has failed). (The word khaba means he was deprived and did not reach what he desired.)

13- Whoever is pleased with the sin of another is an accomplice to the crime. One person killed the camel, but the Quran says a group killed it. "فعقروها" (So they hamstrung her).

14- Allah is dominant over everything and has no fear of the consequence of destroying the oppressors. "ولا یخاف عقباها" (And He does not fear the consequence thereof).

15- Being pleased with, encouraging, and inciting sin is also a sin. "انبعث اشقاها... بذنبهم" (The most wretched of them was incited... because of their sin).

16- Divine punishments are a manifestation of His Lordship (Rububiyyah). (The Nurturer must remove the obstacles to nurturing.) "فدمدم علیهم ربّهم" (So their Lord destroyed them).

17- Divine wrath is severe even in this world. "فدمدم علیهم ربّهم بذنبهم فسوّاها" (So their Lord destroyed them for their sin and leveled it).

74) Ba'atha means he incited, and Inba'atha means he was incited.
